title: When Confidential Compute Is Worth It
summary: A decision framework for separating real confidentiality needs from cases where privacy language sounds compelling but does not materially improve the product or trust model.

bodySections.title: Look for asymmetric downside
bodySections.body: Confidential compute matters most when exposure creates meaningful strategic, financial, or user-trust downside. If revealing the inputs does not change the product in a material way, privacy may not justify the added complexity.

bodySections.title: Look for user-visible value
bodySections.body: The strongest privacy systems improve more than optics. They should create a product experience users can recognize through safer behavior, protected identities, better execution, or new interactions that transparent systems could not support.

bodySections.title: Look for implementation discipline
bodySections.body: A team still needs a clear execution plan, output model, and UX story. Confidentiality is not a substitute for product discipline; it is only worthwhile when the rest of the system is coherent.
